name: Unnamed Location (Spherulitic Chert)
short_description: Public BLM rockhounding site featuring spherulitic chert formations. Gentle terrain near San Francisco Bay Area with accessible collecting of fine chalcedony specimens.
description:
This unnamed BLM location offers direct public access to spherulitic chert formations in the San Francisco Bay Area foothills. The site sits at low elevation on gentle slopes, making it accessible for rockhounders of varying experience levels seeking quality chert and chalcedony specimens.

Spherulitic chert forms through diagenesis of silica-rich sediments, creating distinctive radiating crystal patterns within nodules and layers. The Bay Area's complex geology, shaped by the San Andreas fault system and Cretaceous-Tertiary marine sequences, has produced substantial chert deposits prized by collectors and toolmakers alike.

The terrain here allows for straightforward surface collecting without technical climbing or remote backcountry hiking. You will find loose specimens and weathered nodules scattered across the gentle slope, with chert weathering out of host rock over time. The low elevation and proximity to the Bay means year-round access, though winter rains can temporarily affect parking and footfall.

As BLM land, this location operates under personal use regulations with a 25-pound daily limit. The site remains relatively uncrowded compared to fee dig operations, offering genuine exploration and discovery potential for both beginners and experienced collectors.
history: The San Francisco Bay Area chert deposits formed during the Cretaceous and early Tertiary periods when silica-rich marine sediments accumulated in deep ocean basins. Subsequent subduction and tectonic uplift along the San Andreas fault system exposed these formations, allowing surface weathering to reveal nodular and massive chert bodies that have attracted rockhounders and Native Americans seeking tool-making stone for thousands of years.
tips: Search the gentle slope exposure zones where erosion has exposed fresh chert layers; spherulitic specimens often show distinctive concentric banding when weathered. Bring a loupe to examine the radiating crystal structure and identify higher-quality pieces before packing them in your collection bag. Focus on loose fragments first rather than attempting to extract large nodules, as the surrounding host rock is often quite hard. This site works best as a morning visit followed by sorting specimens at home with a 10x magnifier to study the spherulitic geometry.
terrain_notes: Elevation 114 feet on gentle slopes with mixed grassland and scrub vegetation typical of San Francisco Bay foothills. Relatively flat walking with minimal elevation gain from parking area. Rocky footing with loose chert fragments scattered across surface. No major water features or steep drop-offs. Walking distance from parking likely under 0.5 miles to productive areas.
best_season: October - April. Winter and early spring rains recharge surface exposures and create better digging conditions, while the temperate Bay Area climate allows year-round access. Avoid mid-summer fog and heat that can make prolonged exposure collection uncomfortable on open slopes.
written_directions: From San Francisco, take I-280 south toward San Mateo. The site coordinates place you near the eastern Bay Area foothills access. Approach via local roads toward the coordinates (37.787981, -122.50128) using a GPS device or mapping application. BLM land in this region is accessible via public rights-of-way; confirm current parking area and access points before visiting, as informal parking may shift seasonally.
road_conditions: Approach roads are paved or well-maintained dirt. Check seasonal conditions as unpaved access roads can become slippery during winter rains. Standard sedan suitable for this location.
parking_notes: Informal pullout or turnout parking typical of BLM access areas. Capacity limited to 2-4 vehicles. No facilities or amenities present.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Respect private property boundaries. Pack out all trash.
rules:
Personal use collecting only
Maximum 25 pounds per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection or resale
No motorized excavation or digging equipment
No power tools
Pack out all trash
Respect posted boundaries and private property
nearest_services: San Francisco, approximately 10-15 miles northwest. Gas, food, water, and supplies readily available.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain accessible to most hikers and families with children. Gentle slopes and minimal elevation gain. Unpaved parking area may be uneven. Walking distance to productive areas typically under half a mile with stable footing. Some loose rock fragments present but no major obstacles. Suitable for visitors with moderate mobility.

faq.question: What type of chert can you find at this unnamed San Francisco Bay Area rockhounding site?
faq.answer: This location specializes in spherulitic chert with distinctive radiating crystal patterns and concentric banding. You will also find quality chalcedony specimens and microcrystalline quartz. The spherulitic forms are particularly prized by collectors interested in mineralogy and lapidary work.

faq.question: Do you need a permit to rockhound on this BLM land near San Francisco?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational collecting on BLM land. However, you must follow the 25-pound daily limit per FLPMA and collect for personal use only. Commercial collection is not allowed, and motorized equipment cannot be used.

faq.question: What is the best season for rockhounding spherulitic chert in the San Francisco Bay Area?
faq.answer: October through April is optimal. Winter rains expose fresh specimens and create better digging conditions. The mild Bay Area climate allows year-round access, though summer fog and heat can be uncomfortable for extended collecting on open slopes.

faq.question: Is this San Francisco Bay Area chert site beginner friendly and family safe?
faq.answer: Yes, this location is ideal for beginners and families. The gentle slopes require minimal hiking ability, there are no permits or fees, and children ages 6 and up can participate safely. The easy terrain and straightforward surface collecting make this a perfect introduction to rockhounding.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do you need to collect chert at this Bay Area rockhounding site?
faq.answer: Basic hand tools work well here: bring a hand lens or loupe to examine specimens, a collection bag, and hiking boots with good grip. A small hammer can help crack open weathered nodules to expose interior spherulitic patterns. Avoid motorized equipment as it is prohibited on BLM land.

faq.question: How far is this rockhounding location from San Francisco, and can you bring your dog?
faq.answer: The site is approximately 10-15 miles south of San Francisco via I-280, making it accessible for a day trip. Yes, dogs are allowed on this BLM land, making it an excellent choice for rockhounders who want to combine collecting with outdoor recreation with their pets.

meta_description: Collect spherulitic chert and chalcedony on BLM land near San Francisco. No permit required, 25 lbs daily limit, beginner friendly.
